sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, is a 2-3 week history of a non-healing wound on his right foot, which he attributes to a blister caused by a new pair of Hoka shoes.
He reports no trauma to the foot, no pain, and no other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
The wound is described as a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
He has a history of diabetes with an A1C of over 8 and has not seen a podiatrist in 2 years due to their relocation.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes with an A1C of over 8, and has not seen a podiatrist in 2 years.
He has a history of using a pair of Hoka shoes that caused a blister on his foot.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are: temperature 98.1, heart rate 72, respirations 16, and blood pressure 110/65.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
The wound is not painful to touch.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses.
Doppler signs are present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with no evidence of bone infection, and his diabetes is not well-controlled.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; diabetes mellitus; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed a collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to control his diabetes and lower his A1C.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 2 weeks for follow-up, and may need to be referred to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
